0.opening: January is for clean bright landmarks. The sky is full of obvious anchors, and the cold air often sharpens them into something easy to teach from a porch.

special.title: Cold-air advantage
special.note: On the clearest January nights the stars look harder-edged and steadier, which makes bright patterns easier to learn by eye.

1.opening: February keeps the winter sky strong, but it also hints at the change coming. This is a handoff month between sky seasons.

special.title: Quick-look month
special.note: Short February sky sessions still pay off because the brightest winter stars are already in place as soon as darkness settles.

2.opening: March is when the evening sky visibly pivots. Winter landmarks slide west, spring stars push up, and the equinox changes the whole feel of the after-dark sky.

pattern.title: Winter Triangle to spring handoff
pattern.note: Look early for Sirius and Betelgeuse, then look east for the cleaner spring geometry starting to take over.

planet.title: Alignment-friendly evenings
planet.note: Around the equinox, the evening ecliptic stands taller. If bright planets gather after sunset, March gives them a better stage than flatter-horizon months.

calendar.title: Equinox sky swing
calendar.note: The March equinox changes twilight timing and helps the western dusk sky clear out faster for planet watching.

special.title: Planet-lane lesson
special.note: March is a good month to explain that planets always travel the same sun-road through the sky, even when they spread apart.

3.opening: April is one of the best months for a real sky desk because the geometry is clean, the ecliptic is high, and the first real meteor talk returns.

pattern.title: Spring Triangle reading
pattern.note: Arcturus, Spica, and Regulus help define the season once the winter stars lose their grip on the evening.

planet.title: Planet-pair month
planet.note: If bright planets stack up in the evening sky at all, April is one of the friendlier months for reading their spacing and line by eye.

calendar.title: Lyrid season
calendar.note: Late April brings the Lyrids, which makes this a good month to keep a mental meteor calendar instead of just reacting to headlines.

special.title: Granular sky month
special.note: April rewards people who notice small differences: where the moon sits against bright stars, how planets space out, and how twilight color changes from week to week.

4.opening: May makes the sky work a little harder for your attention. Darkness arrives later, but the twilight pairings can be beautiful if you start looking early enough.

special.title: Twilight pairing season
special.note: May is often better for crescent-moon and bright-planet combinations than for deep-night star work.

5.opening: June is a timing month. The sky is promising, but the late darkness means you need a plan before you step outside.

special.title: Late-dark rule
special.note: The June sky often feels empty if you look too early. Give it enough time and the summer patterns start to earn their keep.

6.opening: July belongs to the Summer Triangle, humid horizons, and late-night sky richness. This is a broad, full season rather than a single-event month.

special.title: Milky Way teaching month
special.note: When the air dries out after a front, July is one of the best times to explain the bright river of the Milky Way without needing equipment.

7.opening: August is when the sky turns social again. The Perseids give people a reason to look up, and the Summer Triangle still keeps the whole sky easy to read.

pattern.title: Summer Triangle overhead
pattern.note: Vega, Deneb, and Altair are still doing most of the teaching work once full dark arrives.

planet.title: Warm-night planet line
planet.note: Any bright planets near the horizon fight August haze, so the cleanest planet views usually come after a dry spell.

calendar.title: Perseid headline month
calendar.note: Mid-August is the annual meteor high point for regular folks, with the best rates usually after midnight and before dawn.

special.title: Lawn-chair astronomy
special.note: August is the month when a blanket, a chair, and patience can beat almost any fancier setup.

8.opening: September starts trading summer spectacle for cleaner air and cleaner structure. The sky gets easier to read once the humidity loosens its grip.

special.title: Cleaner western watch
special.note: September can quietly become one of the better months for evening planet watching if the western horizon stays clear.

9.opening: October skies feel architectural. The Great Square, cooler air, and earlier darkness make this one of the easiest months to point out shapes with confidence.

special.title: Big-shape month
special.note: October is ideal for teaching constellation structure because the humidity falls and the sky feels more orderly.

10.opening: November gives you early darkness and the first real return of winter drama. It is one of the easiest months to fit skywatching into a normal evening.

special.title: Practical observing month
special.note: November lets you get a real sky session without waiting until midnight, which makes it one of the most useful months for habit-building.

11.opening: December puts the drama back in the sky. The big winter stars return, darkness comes early, and the whole thing starts feeling theatrical again.

pattern.title: Winter Triangle comeback
pattern.note: By late December the Winter Triangle is back in force and easy to use as a seasonal anchor.

planet.title: Cold-clear planet season
planet.note: December transparency often gives the crispest naked-eye planet views of the year when the air settles.

calendar.title: Solstice darkness
calendar.note: The longest nights of the year make December a true celestial-calendar month, not just a weather note.

special.title: Holiday porch-sky month
special.note: December is the easiest month to step outside for fifteen minutes and still get a full sky experience.
